myths.Agent_1:
Long ago, before the rugged mountains had voices, there lived a forest nymph named Aethel. Aethel possessed a voice so pure and resonant that the rivers would stall their currents just to listen to her sing. The wind, a proud and jealous spirit named Zephyr, grew envious of the devotion she commanded. He believed that only his gales should provide the music of the valleys.

One twilight, as Aethel sang a gentle lullaby to the setting sun, Zephyr swooped down in a terrible fury. He stole her physical form, scattering her essence across the highest peaks and the deepest canyons, hoping to silence her forever. But the wind is a foolish creature, forgetting that sound does not need a body to survive.

Aethel’s spirit seeped into the very stone of the earth. Now, whenever a lonely traveler calls out into a grand canyon or across a quiet mountain lake, Aethel awakens. She catches the traveler's words, cradles them in her unseen hands, and sings them back. It is her eternal defiance, proving to the jealous wind that a beautiful voice cannot be destroyed. Thus, the echo was born, an immortal reminder of the nymph who became the voice of the mountains.

Every night, he gazed up at the sky, enamored by a magnificent silver star named Lyra. Lyra was the celestial choir master, singing melodies that guided the comets and soothed the restless winds.

Ailil longed to speak to her, to tell her of the frozen waterfalls and the silent pines, but mountain spirits had no voices of their own. They could only shape the earth. Desperate, Ailil carved the canyon walls perfectly smooth, angling them toward the heavens to catch her song. 

One crisp winter night, Lyra sang a note of pure, piercing sorrow. The sound cascaded into the canyon, struck the polished stones, and bounced back up into the sky. Ailil had captured her song and offered it back as a celestial gift.

Startled, Lyra looked down and smiled, realizing she was no longer singing into the void. To this day, Ailil remains in the canyons. He still cannot speak his own words, but whenever a traveler calls out, he faithfully catches their voice and throws it back, waiting for her.
